questions.topper_execution_logic: This tests the fundamental rule of conductors in electrostatic equilibrium. Because all excess charge resides exclusively on the outer surface, any internal Gaussian surface encloses zero net charge, forcing $E = 0$ for all internal points ($r < R$). The moment you step outside ($r \\ge R$), the entire sphere acts exactly like a point charge concentrated at the center, meaning it drops off as an inverse-square relation ($E \\propto r^{-2}$). This makes choice (a) the only correct profile.

questions.associated_trap_title: The Non-Uniform Gauss's Flux Boundary Face Exclusion
questions.stem_text: A rigid closed cube of edge length $a$ is positioned in space with its faces aligned parallel to the coordinate planes. The left face sits at the plane $x = 0$ and the right face sits at the plane $x = a$. The space is filled with a non-uniform electric field vector defined strictly by the equation $\\vec{E} = \\gamma x \\hat{i}$, where $\\gamma$ is a positive constant. What is the net outward electric flux $\\Phi_{\\text{net}}$ passing through the entire cube?

options.a: Zero
options.b: $\\gamma a^{3}$
options.c: $\\gamma a^{2}$
options.d: $2\\gamma a^{3}$

questions.topper_execution_logic: Because the field points exclusively along the x-axis, the area vectors for the top, bottom, front, and back faces are perpendicular to the field lines ($\\theta = 90^{\\circ}$), making their flux contributions zero. You only evaluate the left and right boundaries. At the left face ($x = 0$), the field magnitude is $E_{L} = \\gamma(0) = 0$, so flux is zero. At the right face ($x = a$), the field magnitude is $E_{R} = \\gamma a$, and the outward normal points right, yielding $\\Phi_{R} = (\\gamma a) \\cdot a^{2} = \\gamma a^{3}$. Adding them up gives exactly $\\gamma a^{3}$, matching choice (b).

questions.stem_text:
Review the following statements regarding the physical characteristics of electrostatic field lines: 

Statement $1$: Electrostatic field lines can form continuous closed loops in a charge-free region. 
Statement $2$: The relative crowding density of field lines in a localized region indicates the relative strength of the electric field magnitude there. 
Statement $3$: Two field lines can cross each other if the source charges possess highly unequal magnitudes. 

Which of these statements $is/are$ entirely true?

options.a: Statement $1$ and Statement $2$ only
options.b: Statement $2$ only
options.c: Statement $2$ and Statement $3$ only
options.d: Statement $1$, Statement $2$, and Statement $3$

questions.topper_execution_logic: Run a quick elimination check. Statement $1$ is completely false because electrostatic field lines never form closed loops due to the conservative nature of the field. Statement $3$ is also completely false because field lines *never* cross under any circumstance; doing so would create two distinct tangents at the intersection point, ruining vector uniqueness. Only Statement $2$ is a verified textbook fact: crowded lines mean a strong field, sparse lines mean a weak field. Choice (b) is the clear winner.

questions.stem_text: A short electric dipole with a permanent dipole moment magnitude $p$ generates an electric field magnitude of $E_{1}$ at a far-away axial position located at a distance $r$ from its center. The same dipole creates an electric field magnitude of $E_{2}$ at a far-away equatorial plane coordinate located at a distance of $2r$ from its center. Under the definitive point-dipole condition ($r \\gg a$), what is the exact value of the numerical ratio $\\frac{E_{1}}{E_{2}}$?
questions.topper_execution_logic: Don't fall for the simple ratio trap of $2$ by assuming same-distance points! Write out the short-dipole scaling equations carefully: $E_{1} = E_{\\text{axial}}(r) = \\frac{2kp}{r^{3}}$. For the equatorial point at twice the distance, $E_{2} = E_{\\text{equatorial}}(2r) = \\frac{kp}{(2r)^{3}} = \\frac{kp}{8r^{3}}$. Now divide them: $\\frac{E_{1}}{E_{2}} = \\frac{2kp/r^{3}}{kp/8r^{3}} = 2 \\times 8 = 16$. The inverse-cubic drop-off behavior turns the doubled distance into an extra factor of $8$, pushing the final ratio up to $16$.

questions.stem_text: An electric dipole consists of two point charges $+1 \\mu{\\text{C}}$ and $-1 \\mu{\\text{C}}$, held $10$ cm apart. It is subjected to a uniform electric field of $100 N/C$. Calculate the amount of work done in turning the dipole from its position of stable equilibrium to the position of unstable equilibrium, in the field.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Identification of initial stable orientation angle $\\theta_{1} = 0^{\\circ}$ and final unstable orientation angle $\\theta_{2} = 180^{\\circ}$, combined with the general work equation.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: Stable equilibrium is at $\\theta_{1} = 0^{\\circ}$ and unstable equilibrium is at $\\theta_{2} = 180^{\\circ}$. Work done is $W = pE(\\cos\\theta_{1} - \\cos\\theta_{2}) = 2pE$.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Accurate calculation of the dipole moment $p$ and final numeric work value with appropriate S.I. units.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: Dipole moment $p = q(2a) = 10^{-6}\\text{ C} \\times 0.1\\text{ m} = 10^{-7}\\text{ C}\\cdot\\text{m}$. Net work $W = 2 \\times 10^{-7} \\times 100 = 2 \\times 10^{-5}\\text{ J}$.

questions.topper_execution_logic: Watch out for the classic trap here! The $10$ cm separation given is the *entire* dipole length (2a), not just 'a'. If you mistake it for 'a' and double it, your dipole moment doubles, pushing your work value to $4 \\times 10^{-5}\\text{ J}$, which triggers an automatic $-0.5$ mark penalty. Also, never forget to append the unit symbol ('J' or $'N\\cdot\\text{m}$') at the end, or examiners will slice off another $0.5$ marks instantly.

questions.stem_text: Four point charges of $1 \\mu{\\text{C}}, -2 \\mu{\\text{C}}, 1 \\mu{\\text{C}}$ and $-2 \\mu{\\text{C}}$ are placed at the corners $A, B, C$ and D respectively, of a square of side $30$ cm. Find the net force acting on a charge of $4 \\mu{\\text{C}}$ placed at the centre of the square.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Vector resolution and pair-wise cancellation along diagonal AC.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: Forces exerted on the central $+4 \\mu{\\text{C}}$ charge by equal charges at opposite corners A and $C (1 \\mu{\\text{C}}$ each) are equal in magnitude but opposite in direction. They cancel vectorially: $\\vec{F}_{A} + \\vec{F}_{C} = 0\\text{ N}$.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Vector cancellation along diagonal BD and stating the final net force.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: Similarly, charges at corners B and $D (-2 \\mu{\\text{C}}$ each) exert equal and opposite attractive forces on the center charge, canceling vectorially: $\\vec{F}_{B} + \\vec{F}_{D} = 0\\text{ N}$. Hence, $\\vec{F}_{\\text{net}} = 0\\text{ N}$.

questions.topper_execution_logic: Don't waste time running elaborate, tedious calculations for diagonal distances and individual field strengths. Examiners are strictly trained to look for direct, symmetry-based vector cancellations. Explicitly state that pairs of equal charges at opposite corners cancel out vectorially. If you write a long mathematical derivation and introduce a tiny arithmetic slip up that yields a non-zero net vector, you forfeit the entire second mark automatically.

questions.stem_text: Two point charges of $3 \\mu{\\text{C}}$ and $4 \\mu{\\text{C}}$ are kept in air at ($0.3 m, 0$) and ($0, 0.3 m$) in $x-y$ plane. Find the magnitude and direction of the net electric field produced at the origin ($0, 0$).

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Computing the component electric fields $E_{x}$ and $E_{y}$ at the origin using Coulomb's constant, including direction vectors.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: Field due to $q_{1}$ is $E_{x} = \\frac{1}{4\\pi\\epsilon_{0}} \\frac{q_{1}}{x^{2}}$ along $-\\hat{i}$, and field due to $q_{2}$ is $E_{y} = \\frac{1}{4\\pi\\epsilon_{0}} \\frac{q_{2}}{y^{2}}$ along $-\\hat{j}$.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Vector addition of components to find net magnitude and coordinate direction.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: $E_{\\text{net}} = \\sqrt{E_{x}^{2} + E_{y}^{2}} = 5 \\times 10^{5}\\text{ N/C}$. Direction is $\\theta = \\tan^{-1}(E_{y}/E_{x}) = \\tan^{-1}(4/3) = 53.1^{\circ}$ relative to the negative x-axis into the third quadrant.

questions.topper_execution_logic: Finding the net magnitude is only half the battle. The major deduction trap here is failing to mathematically define the coordinate direction angle $\\theta$. Writing conversational directional phrases like 'direction is southwest' will immediately get you docked $-0.5$ marks. Always compute the exact angle via $\\tan\\theta = E_{y}/E_{x}$ and explicitly specify which axis it is measured from to lock down full points.

questions.stem_text: Two point charges $q_{A} = 3 \\mu{\\text{C}}$ and $q_{B} = -3 \\mu{\\text{C}}$ are located $20$ cm apart in vacuum. Find the magnitude and direction of the electric field at the midpoint O of the line AB joining the two charges.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Stating that fields due to both positive and negative charges point in the same direction along line AB and identifying the correct midpoint distance $r$.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: Midpoint distance $r = 10$ cm $= 0.1 m$. The electric field due to the positive charge points away from A (towards O), and the field due to the negative charge points towards B (towards O). Both vectors point in the same direction along OB.
algebraic_progression: $\\vec{E}_{A} \\text{ and } \\vec{E}_{B} \\text{ are both directed towards the right along } \\vec{OB}$

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Calculating the individual field magnitudes and taking their algebraic sum with correct S.I. units and direction description.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: $E_{\\text{net}} = E_{A} + E_{B} = 2 \\times \\frac{1}{4\\pi\\epsilon_{0}}\\frac{q}{r^{2}} = 5.4 \\times 10^{6}\\text{ N/C}$ directed along OB towards the negative charge.

questions.topper_execution_logic: Do not instinctively write zero just because the charge magnitudes are equal! Because the charges have opposite signs, their field contributions at the midpoint cooperate and point in the exact same direction (towards the negative charge), rather than canceling out. Also, be careful to use the half-distance $r = 10$ cm $= 0.1 m$ instead of the total separation distance $20$ cm, or your entire numerical calculation collapses.

questions.stem_text: An electric dipole with a permanent dipole moment of $4 \\times 10^{-9} C\\cdot{}m$ is aligned at $30^{\\circ}$ with the direction of a uniform external electric field of magnitude $5 \\times 10^{4} N/C$. Calculate the magnitude of the torque acting on the dipole.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Stating the correct vector formula for torque on an electric dipole in a uniform field and substituting the given parameters.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: Torque experienced by a dipole is given by $\\tau = pE\\sin\\theta$. Substituting the values: $p = 4 \\times 10^{-9}\\text{ C}\\cdot\\text{m}, E = 5 \\times 10^{4}\\text{ N/C}$, and $\\theta = 30^{\\circ}$.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Evaluating the mathematical expression correctly to arrive at the final numeric value with proper S.I. units.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: $\\tau = 20 \\times 10^{-5} \\times 0.5 = 10^{-4}\\text{ N}\\cdot\\text{m}$.

questions.topper_execution_logic: This question is a total gift, but it is an absolute magnet for sloppy unit penalties. The standard unit for torque is $\\text{N}\\cdot\\text{m}$ (Newton-meter). If you leave it unitless or write something ridiculous like $\\text{N/C}$, the examiner is contractually obligated to dock you $-0.5$ marks. Double-check that you remember $\\sin 30^{\\circ} = 0.5$ correctly so an absolute brain-fade arithmetic mistake doesn't steal your second mark.

questions.stem_text:
(a) Twelve negative charges of same magnitude $-q$ are equally spaced and fixed on the circumference of a circle of radius $R$. Relative to potential being zero at infinity, find the electric potential and electric field at the centre $C$ of the circle.

(b) If the same twelve negative charges are unequally spaced and fixed along an arc of $120^{\\circ}$ of radius $R$, find the new electric potential at the centre $C$.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Determining the net electric field at the center of the symmetrical circle via vector symmetry arguments.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: Due to symmetric distribution of the $12$ identical charges on the circumference, the vector sum of all electric fields at the center is zero: $\\vec{E} = 0\\text{ N/C}$.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Calculating the scalar sum of the electric potential at the center for the complete circle.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: Potential is a scalar quantity. The total potential at the center is the algebraic sum of the potentials of the $12$ negative charges: $V = 12 \\times \\left(-\\frac{q}{4\\pi\\epsilon_{0}R}\\right) = -\\frac{3q}{\\pi\\epsilon_{0}R}$.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Deducing the potential for the arc configuration based on scalar properties.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: Since potential is a scalar quantity, it is independent of the angular spacing of the charges. Because all $12$ negative charges remain at distance $R$ from the center, the potential is unchanged: $V = -\\frac{3q}{\\pi\\epsilon_{0}R}$.

questions.topper_execution_logic: Examiners absolutely love to trick you here into vectorially resolving the potential for part (b). Don't fall for it! Potential is a scalar, so it doesn't give a damn about angular spacing—only distance matters. The single biggest trap is forgetting the negative sign for the negative charges; writing a positive potential will vaporize $1.0$ full mark instantly.

questions.stem_text: Two small solid metal balls A and B of radii $R$ and $2R$ having initial surface charge densities $2\\sigma$ and $3\\sigma$ respectively are kept far apart. Find the final surface charge densities on A and B after they are connected by a conducting wire.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Formulating initial individual charges and calculating the total system charge $Q$.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: Initial charges are $q_{A} = 2\\sigma(4\\pi R^{2}) = 8\\pi R^{2}\\sigma$ and $q_{B} = 3\\sigma(4\\pi(2R)^{2}) = 48\\pi R^{2}\\sigma$. Total charge $Q = q_{A} + q_{B} = 56\\pi R^{2}\\sigma$.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Formulating the potential equality condition at equilibrium to establish the final charge ratio.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: When connected, charge flows until potentials equalize: $V_{A} = V_{B} \\implies \\frac{q'_{A}}{4\\pi\\epsilon_{0}R} = \\frac{q'_{B}}{4\\pi\\epsilon_{0}(2R)} \\implies q'_{B} = 2q'_{A}$.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Solving the system of linear equations to compute the final surface charge densities.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: Since $q'_{A} + q'_{B} = Q \\implies 3q'_{A} = 56\\pi R^{2}\\sigma \\implies q'_{A} = \\frac{56}{3}\\pi R^{2}\\sigma$. New charge densities are $\\sigma'_{A} = \\frac{q'_{A}}{4\\pi R^{2}} = \\frac{14}{3}\\sigma$ and $\\sigma'_{B} = \\frac{q'_{B}}{4\\pi(2R)^{2}} = \\frac{7}{3}\\sigma$.
algebraic_progression: $3q'_{A} = 56\\pi R^{2}\\sigma \\implies q'_{A} = \\frac{56}{3}\\pi R^{2}\\sigma \\text{ and } q'_{B} = \\frac{112}{3}\\pi R^{2}\\sigma$

questions.topper_execution_logic: The absolute execution trap here is assuming that charges divide equally ($q'_{A} = q'_{B}$) when the spheres are connected. They don't! It's the *potentials* that become equal ($V_{A} = V_{B}$). If you mix this up, your steps $2$ and $3$ drop to zero instantly, capping your score at a measly $1$ out of $3$. Keep your variables crystal clear.

questions.stem_text: Three point charges, $1 \\mu{\\text{C}}$ each, are kept at the vertices of an equilateral triangle of side $10$ cm. Find the net electric field at the centroid of the triangle.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Calculating the correct geometric distance $r$ from the vertices to the triangle's centroid.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: Altitude AD $= 10 \\cos(30^{\\circ}) = 5\\sqrt{3}\\text{$ cm}. Distance from vertex to centroid is $r = \\frac{2}{3}AD = \\frac{10}{\\sqrt{3}}\\text{$ cm} $= \\frac{0.1}{\\sqrt{3}}\\text{ m}$.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Computing individual field magnitudes at the centroid using Coulomb's constant.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: Individual field magnitudes are equal: $E_{A} = E_{B} = E_{C} = \\frac{1}{4\\pi\\epsilon_{0}}\\frac{q}{r^{2}} = 9 \\times 10^{9} \\times \\frac{10^{-6}}{(0.1/\\sqrt{3})^{2}} = 2.7 \\times 10^{6}\\text{ N/C}$.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Resolving vector components at the centroid to show perfect symmetric cancellation.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: The resultant of $\\vec{E}_{B}$ and $\\vec{E}_{C}$, oriented at $120^{\\circ}$ relative to each other, has a magnitude of $2.7 \\times 10^{6}\\text{ N/C}$ along the angle bisector, which is equal and opposite to $\\vec{E}_{A}$. Thus, $\\vec{E}_{\\text{net}} = 0\\text{ N/C}$.

questions.topper_execution_logic: Calculating the centroid distance wrong (like using $r = a$ or $r = a/2$) is the most frequent blunder here. Also, even if your final answer is $0$, examiners will slash $-0.5$ marks if you don't back it up with a clear sentence explaining how the 120-degree vector pairs cancel out along the angle bisector. A quick vector sketch on the margin is your best defense.

questions.stem_text:
A uniform electric field is given by $\\vec{E} = 200\\hat{i}\\text{ N/C}$ for $x > 0$ and $\\vec{E} = -200\\hat{i}\\text{ N/C}$ for $x < 0. A$ right circular cylinder of length $20$ cm and radius $5$ cm has its center at the origin and its axis along the x-axis. Calculate:

(a) the net outward flux through each flat face, and

(b) the flux through the curved side of the cylinder.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Computing the outward electric flux through the left flat face with correct area alignment.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: On the left face ($x = -10$ cm), $\\vec{E}$ and outward normal $\\hat{n}_{L}$ are both along $-\\hat{i}$ (parallel). Flux $\\phi_{L} = E\\Delta S = 200 \\times \\pi(0.05)^{2} = +1.57\\text{ N}\\cdot{}m^{2}/C$.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Computing the outward electric flux through the right flat face with correct area alignment.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: On the right face ($x = +10$ cm), $\\vec{E}$ and outward normal $\\hat{n}_{R}$ are both along $+\\hat{i}$ (parallel). Flux $\\phi_{R} = E\\Delta S = 200 \\times \\pi(0.05)^{2} = +1.57\\text{ N}\\cdot{}m^{2}/C$.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Evaluating the flux through the curved side of the cylinder by citing perpendicular vector orientations.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: For any point on the curved side of the cylinder, $\\vec{E}$ is perpendicular to the area elements $d\\vec{A}$, so $\\vec{E} \\cdot d\\vec{A} = 0$. Therefore, the flux through the curved part is $\\phi_{C} = 0\\text{ N}\\cdot{}m^{2}/C$.

questions.topper_execution_logic: This question targets the Boundary Face Exclusion Trap. Many students blindly add the coordinates or think the fields cancel because one is positive and one is negative. Remember, outward normal vectors *always* point away from the interior of a closed volume. Thus, on the left face, the area vector points left ($-\\hat{i}$), making the dot product positive! Don't let opposite field signs fool you into a zero-flux error.

questions.stem_text:
An electric dipole of permanent dipole moment $\\vec{p}$ is placed in a non-uniform electric field $\\vec{E}$ such that $\\vec{p}$ is aligned parallel to the direction of the field gradient.

(a) What is the net torque acting on the dipole?

(b) Describe the magnitude and direction of the net translational force experienced by this dipole.

(c) What changes if $\\vec{p}$ is oriented anti-parallel to the field gradient?

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Evaluating the net torque acting on the dipole under parallel alignment conditions.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: Since $\\vec{p}$ is parallel to $\\vec{E}$, the angle $\\theta = 0^{\\circ}$. The torque is $\\vec{\\tau} = \\vec{p} \\times \\vec{E} \\implies \\tau = pE\\sin(0^{\\circ}) = 0\\text{ N}\\cdot{}m$.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Deducing the translational force direction for the parallel configuration.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: In a non-uniform field, the forces on $+q$ and $-q$ do not cancel. When parallel, the positive charge experiences a stronger field than the negative charge, creating a net translational force in the direction of the increasing electric field.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Deducing the translational force shift under anti-parallel alignment conditions.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: When anti-parallel ($\\theta = 180^{\\circ}$), torque remains zero as $\\sin(180^{\\circ}) = 0$. However, the negative charge $-q$ is now positioned in the stronger region of the field, so the net translational force points in the direction of the decreasing field.
algebraic_progression: $\\vec{F}_{\\text{net}} \\text{ acts anti-parallel to the field gradient (decreasing field)}$

questions.topper_execution_logic: Students memorize that 'net force on a dipole is zero' from uniform fields and blindly dump that fact here. Read carefully: this is a *non-uniform* field! The net torque is zero because of the parallel/anti-parallel alignment, but the translational force is absolutely non-zero. Explicitly specify the words 'direction of increasing field' and 'direction of decreasing field' to win full marks from the marking rubric.

questions.stem_text:
Read the following passage and answer the questions that follow:

The behavior of electric dipoles in external electric fields is fundamental to understanding dielectric materials and electrostatic shielding. A molecule is a polar molecule if its center of positive charges does not coincide with the center of negative charges, creating a permanent dipole moment $\\vec{p}$. When placed in a uniform electric field $\\vec{E}, a$ dipole experiences a net torque $\\vec{\\tau} = \\vec{p} \\times \\vec{E}$ but no translational force. However, in a non-uniform field, it experiences both torque and a net translational force due to the field gradient.

(a) A polar molecule with an intrinsic dipole moment of $3.0 \\times 10^{-30} C\\cdot{}m$ is placed in a uniform electric field of $2.0 \\times 10^{4} N/C$. Calculate the maximum torque experienced by the molecule. ($1$ Mark)

(b) If the same molecule is oriented parallel to the uniform electric field, what is the net force and net torque acting on it? ($1$ Mark)

(c) Two identical small electric dipoles, each of dipole moment magnitude $p$, are arranged perpendicular to each other such that their negative charges coincide at the origin. Find the magnitude and direction of the net dipole moment of this arrangement. ($2$ Marks)

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Stating the maximum torque condition ($\\sin\\theta = 1$) and substituting parameters accurately.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: Torque is maximum when $\\theta = 90^{\\circ} \\implies \\tau_{\\text{max}} =$ pE. Substituting values gives $\\tau_{\\text{max}} = (3.0 \\times 10^{-30}) \\times (2.0 \\times 10^{4}) = 6.0 \\times 10^{-26} N\\cdot{}m$.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Evaluating both force and torque for parallel orientation in a uniform field.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: When parallel ($\\theta = 0^{\\circ}$) in a uniform field, net torque $\\tau = pE\\sin(0^{\\circ}) = 0 N\\cdot{}m$, and net translational force $F_{\\text{net}} = 0 N$ because forces on equal opposite charges cancel.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Applying vector addition for perpendicular dipoles and computing final magnitude and angular direction.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: Since the dipoles are mutually perpendicular, their moments add vectorially: $p_{\\text{net}} = \\sqrt{p^{2} + p^{2}} = \\sqrt{2}p$. The direction forms an angle $\\theta = \\tan^{-1}(p/p) = 45^{\\circ}$ bisecting the two dipole axes.

questions.topper_execution_logic: In part (b), a classic mistake is forgetting to write down BOTH values; if you miss stating that the net force is zero alongside the torque, you'll get hit with a $-0.5$ mark deduction. For part (c), calculating just the magnitude ($\\sqrt{2}p$) only gets you $1$ mark. You must explicitly calculate and state the angle ($45^{\\circ}$) relative to one of the component vectors to lock down the final mark.

questions.stem_text:
Read the following passage and answer the questions that follow:

Gauss's law provides a highly efficient tool to calculate fields under strict symmetric layouts. It states that the net outward electric flux through any closed boundary surface equals the total enclosed net charge divided by the permittivity of free space $\\epsilon_{0}$. However, when evaluating spaces containing fields with strong spatial gradients, the flux must be evaluated face-by-face over separate cross-sectional planes, tracking how area normals align with field lines.

(a) Consider electric field components inside a spatial grid given by $E_{x} = \\alpha{}x^{1/2}$ and $E_{y} = E_{z} = 0$, where $\\alpha = 800 N/(C\\cdot{}m^{1/2}$). Why is the electric flux through the top and bottom faces of a cube aligned parallel to the coordinate planes equal to zero? ($1$ Mark)

(b) Calculate the net outward flux through a cube of edge length a $= 0.1 m$ if its left boundary sits at $x = a$ and its right boundary sits at $x = 2a. (2$ Marks)

(c) Using Gauss's law, determine the total net charge contained within this cubic enclosure. ($1$ Mark)

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Stating that the electric field is perpendicular to the area vector elements of the parallel boundary faces.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: Since $E_{y} = E_{z} = 0$, the field vector $\\vec{E}$ points purely along the x-direction. The outward area vectors of the top and bottom faces point along the y-axis ($\\pm\\hat{j}$), making them perpendicular to $\\vec{E} (\\theta = 90^{\\circ}$), hence flux is zero.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Setting up separate negative and positive flux contributions for the opposite faces and solving the algebraic assembly.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: Left face flux $\\phi_{L} = -E_{L}a^{2} = -\\alpha a^{5/2}$. Right face flux $\\phi_{R} = E_{R}a^{2} = \\alpha(2a)^{1/2}a^{2} = \\sqrt{2}\\alpha a^{5/2}$. Net flux $\\phi_{\\text{net}} = \\alpha a^{5/2}(\\sqrt{2} - 1) = 800 \\times (0.1)^{5/2} \\times 0.414 = 1.05\\text{ N}\\cdot\\text{m}^{2}/\\text{C}$.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Applying the isolated charge-flux equation from Gauss's law with correct values.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: By Gauss's law, $q = \\phi_{\\text{net}}\\epsilon_{0}$. Substituting the computed flux gives $q = 1.05 \\times 8.854 \\times 10^{-12} = 9.27 \\times 10^{-12}\\text{ C}$.

questions.topper_execution_logic: This question tests the Boundary Face Exclusion Trap. The biggest blunder here is getting the sign wrong on the left face flux. Because the field points right ($+\\hat{i}$) and the outward normal of the left face points left ($-\\hat{i}$), the dot product forces a negative sign. If you blindly add both face fluxes without noting that $\\phi_{L}$ is negative, your total math will tank, and you'll drop down to zero for parts (b) and (c).

questions.stem_text:
Read the following passage and answer the questions that follow:

The calculation of electric field profiles for infinite continuous charge lines relies on isolating symmetrical surfaces. For an infinitely long straight wire with uniform linear charge density $\\lambda$, the electric field exhibits a purely radial distribution perpendicular to the wire axis. To isolate this mathematically, a coaxial cylinder of length $l$ and radius $r$ is mapped around the wire line as a closed imaginary Gaussian boundary.

(a) Write the expression for the total electric flux passing through the two flat end caps of this cylindrical Gaussian surface, providing a strict physical reason. ($1$ Mark)

(b) Derive the explicit algebraic formula for the electric field intensity $E$ at a radial distance $r$ from this infinitely long straight uniformly charged wire using Gauss's law. ($2$ Marks)

(c) If the radial distance $r$ from the line charge is exactly doubled, by what factor does the electric field magnitude change? ($1$ Mark)

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Stating that flux through flat boundaries is zero because field vectors run parallel to those faces.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: The flux is $0$. Since the field $\\vec{E}$ is directed radially outwards, it is perpendicular to the area vectors $d\\vec{A}$ of the flat caps ($\\theta = 90^{\\circ}$), causing the dot product to vanish.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Equating the calculated curved surface flux integral to the enclosed linear charge expression via Gauss's law.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: Curved surface flux is $\\phi = E(2\\pi r l$). Enclosed charge is $q = \\lambda l$. By Gauss's law, $E(2\\pi r l) = \\frac{\\lambda l}{\\epsilon_{0}} \\implies E = \\frac{\\lambda}{2\\pi\\epsilon_{0}r}$.
algebraic_progression: $\\oint \\vec{E} \\cdot d\\vec{A} = E \\int dA = E(2\\pi r l)$

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Identifying the linear inverse scaling relationship between the field and the radius parameter.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: Since $E$ is inversely proportional to $r (E \\propto 1/r$), doubling the distance halves the field strength: $E' = E/2$.

questions.topper_execution_logic: Be extremely careful in part (c). Students get so conditioned to Coulomb's inverse-square law ($1/r^{2}$) that they instinctively write 'decreases by $4$ times' without thinking. A line charge has an electric field that drops off linearly ($1/r$). Make sure you highlight that specific scaling rule, or you'll hand over an easy point to the examiner.

questions.stem_text:
Read the following passage and answer the questions that follow:

Electrostatic layouts involving combinations of point charges require vector component tracking to resolve the net electric fields. When multiple point charges of identical magnitude and sign are distributed symmetrically across the vertices of a regular geometric array, the vector field contributions at the exact geometric center often cancel out cleanly due to angular symmetries. However, replacing even a single charge within the array disrupts this equilibrium, turning the center into a highly active field coordinate.

(a) Three identical point charges of $+q$ are fixed at the three vertices of an equilateral triangle of side length $l$. State the net electric field vector at the centroid O of this symmetric system and justify your answer using symmetry concepts. ($1$ Mark)

(b) If a negative point charge $-Q$ is placed precisely at the centroid location O, calculate the magnitude of the net electrostatic force acting on it. ($1$ Mark)

(c) If one of the point charges at a vertex is completely replaced by a negative charge of equal magnitude $-q$, calculate the revised magnitude of the net electric field at the centroid O in terms of $q$ and $l. (2$ Marks)

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Stating that symmetric vector arrays at equal distances sum up to zero at the geometric center.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: The net electric field vector is zero ($\\vec{E}_{\\text{net}} = 0$). Due to the completely symmetric placement of equal charges at identical distances from the centroid, their individual field vectors cancel out vectorially.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Applying the core relationship between field vector values and localized force calculations.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: The net force is zero ($F = 0$). Since the net electric field intensity at the centroid location is exactly zero, the force experienced by any charge at that position is $F = qE_{\\text{net}} = 0 N$.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Calculating vertex-centroid distances and processing the component addition after changing vector signs.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: Distance to centroid is $r = l/\\sqrt{3}$. Resultant of the remaining two positive vertex fields has magnitude $E = \\frac{q}{4\\pi\\epsilon_{0}r^{2}}$ directed toward the replaced vertex. The replaced negative charge also produces a field of magnitude $E$ in that same direction. They add directly: $E_{\\text{net}} = 2E = 2\\left(\\frac{3q}{4\\pi\\epsilon_{0}l^{2}}\\right) = \\frac{6q}{4\\pi\\epsilon_{0}l^{2}}$.

questions.topper_execution_logic: This question showcases the Vector Geometry Mutation trick. In part (c), flipping a charge's sign transforms a safe cancellation problem into an additive combination. The fields no longer cancel out; instead, the vectors reinforce each other along the single line of symmetry. Make sure you state clearly that both field components align in the *same direction* to claim full step marks for the derivation.

questions.stem_text:
Read the following passage and answer the questions that follow:

A uniformly charged thin spherical shell exhibits field profiles that transition sharply between its interior and exterior boundaries. According to Gauss's law, for any point located outside the shell structure, the field acts as if the entire charge on the boundary surface were concentrated completely at its geometric center. Conversely, tracking points inside the shell cavity reveals a field-free zone due to the total absence of enclosed charge profiles within the internal boundary.

(a) State the electric field intensity value at any point inside a uniformly charged thin spherical shell of radius $R$ at a distance $r < R$ from the center, citing the underlying physical reason. ($1$ Mark)

(b) Using a spherical Gaussian surface of radius $r > R$, derive the expression for the electric field at an external point outside the shell boundary. ($2$ Marks)

(c) Describe what happens to the electric field intensity value at the exact boundary transition where $r = R. (1$ Mark)

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Declaring a zero field state internally due to the lack of enclosed charge inside the surface.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: $E = 0$. Because a Gaussian surface drawn inside the shell cavity ($r < R$) encloses zero net charge ($q = 0$), Gauss's law forces the field magnitude to drop to zero.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Integrating the full outer surface boundary area and linking it directly to the total surface charge.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: For a Gaussian sphere of radius $r > R$, the field $\\vec{E}$ is uniform and parallel to area vectors. Total flux $\\phi = E(4\\pi r^{2}$). Enclosed charge is $q$. Gauss's law gives $E(4\\pi r^{2}) = \\frac{q}{\\epsilon_{0}} \\implies E = \\frac{q}{4\\pi\\epsilon_{0}r^{2}}$.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Describing the sharp discontinuous jump behavior of the field function at the shell surface.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: At $r = R$, the electric field experiences a discontinuous jump from zero inside to its maximum value, $E_{\\text{max}} = \\frac{q}{4\\pi\\epsilon_{0}R^{2}}$, right on the outer skin of the shell.

questions.topper_execution_logic: This question maps right to the Core Counterpart Extraction protocol. The classic trap here is glossing over the boundary description at $r = R$ in part (c). Do not just say 'it is equal to the formula'. Examiners award points specifically for highlighting the discontinuous jump behavior. Explicitly stating that the field jumps from zero inside to a maximum value right on the skin will net you full marks.

questions.stem_text:
(a) (i) State Coulomb's law in electrostatics and write it in vector form for two charges.

(ii) 'Gauss's law is based on the inverse-square dependence on distance contained in Coulomb's law.' Explain this statement.

(b) Two charges A (charge $q$) and B (charge $2q$) are located at points ($0, 0$) and ($a, a$) respectively in the $x-y$ plane. Let $\\hat{i}$ and $\\hat{j}$ be the unit vectors along the x-axis and y-axis respectively. Find the net electrostatic force exerted by A on B in terms of $\\hat{i}$ and $\\hat{j}$.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Accurate scalar definition statement of Coulomb's law accompanied by a fully correct vector notation expression.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: The electrostatic force between two point charges is proportional to the product of charges and inversely proportional to the square of their distance. In vector notation: $\\vec{F}_{12} = \\frac{1}{4\\pi\\epsilon_{0}} \\frac{q_{1}q_{2}}{r_{12}^{2}} \\hat{r}_{12}$.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Linking the geometric surface area of a Gaussian sphere to the inverse-square field drop-off to prove flux independence.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: The field of a point charge is $E = \\frac{q}{4\\pi\\epsilon_{0}r^{2}}$. Flux through a concentric sphere is $\\Phi = E \\cdot (4\\pi r^{2}) = \\frac{q}{\\epsilon_{0}}$. The $r^{2}$ terms cancel out perfectly, proving flux is independent of the radius.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Extracting the correct position vector and calculating its exact distance magnitude.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: The relative position vector running from A to B is $\\vec{r}_{AB} = a\\hat{i} + a\\hat{j}$. The linear separation distance magnitude is $r = \\sqrt{a^{2} + a^{2}} = a\\sqrt{2}$.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Substituting charge identities and position parameters accurately into Coulomb's vector equation.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: Substituting $q_{1} = q, q_{2} = 2q$, and unit direction vector $\\hat{r}$ into Coulomb's law equation: $\\vec{F}_{AB} = \\frac{1}{4\\pi\\epsilon_{0}} \\frac{q(2q)}{(a\\sqrt{2})^{2}} \\left(\\frac{a\\hat{i} + a\\hat{j}}{a\\sqrt{2}}\\right$).

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Simplifying the expressions into a finalized vector format with correct S.I. units.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: Simplifying variables yields the final force expression: $\\vec{F}_{AB} = \\frac{1}{4\\pi\\epsilon_{0}} \\frac{q^{2}}{\\sqrt{2}a^{2}} (\\hat{i} + \\hat{j})\\text{ N}$.

questions.topper_execution_logic: Watch out for the massive vector notation trap that examiners actively weaponize! If you write a displacement vector $\\vec{r}$ in the numerator, your denominator *must* be $r^{3}$ to keep the dimensions valid. Writing $r^{2}$ with a full vector in the numerator is a catastrophic dimensional error that triggers an automatic $-1.0$ mark penalty. Also, ensure you compute the position separation as $a\\sqrt{2}$ instead of $2a^{2}$ to prevent messy coordinate shifts.

questions.stem_text:
(a) Derive an expression for the electric field at a point on the equatorial plane of an electric dipole consisting of charges $q$ and $-q$ separated by a distance $2a$.

(b) Two point charges $+q$ and $-q$ are held at ($a, 0$) and ($-a, 0$) in the $x-y$ plane. Obtain an expression for the net electric field due to these charges at a point ($0, y$). Hence, find the expression for the electric field in the far-field limit where $y \\gg a$.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Resolving electric field vectors into spatial components and demonstrating vertical cancellation.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: Vertical components $E_{+} \\sin\\theta$ and $E_{-} \\sin\\theta$ are equal and opposite, so they cancel out completely. Horizontal components $E_{+} \\cos\\theta$ and $E_{-} \\cos\\theta$ align in the same direction and add up.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Assembling the full algebraic expression by substituting the correct distance and cosine formulas.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: Substitute $E_{+} = \\frac{q}{4\\pi\\epsilon_{0}(y^{2}+a^{2})}$ and $\\cos\\theta = \\frac{a}{(y^{2}+a^{2})^{1/2}}$. This yields $E_{\\text{net}} = \\frac{2qa}{4\\pi\\epsilon_{0}(y^{2}+a^{2})^{3/2}} = \\frac{p}{4\\pi\\epsilon_{0}(y^{2}+a^{2})^{3/2}}$.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Applying the far-field approximation criteria and expressing the field as a functional vector.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: For $y \\gg a, a^{2}$ can be neglected entirely. The vector expression is $\\vec{E}_{\\text{eq}} = -\\frac{\\vec{p}}{4\\pi\\epsilon_{0}y^{3}}$, showing it points opposite to the dipole moment vector.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Deducing field scaling behavior when distance coordinates are halved using inverse-cube properties.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: Since $E \\propto 1/y^{3}$, halving the distance parameter $y$ increases the field strength by a factor of $2^{3} = 8$ times, giving $E' = 8E$.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Proving the relative directional orientation between the field and dipole moment vectors.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: The dipole moment points from $-q$ to $+q$ (along $+\\hat{i}$), whereas the net field at the equatorial coordinate points along $-\\hat{i}$. Thus, the field is strictly anti-parallel to the dipole moment vector.

questions.topper_execution_logic: Dropping the negative sign in your final vector equation for $\\vec{E}_{\\text{eq}}$ is an automatic $-0.5$ mark deduction trap. Human examiners check specifically for that minus sign because it proves you understand that the equatorial field points anti-parallel to the dipole moment vector $\\vec{p}$. Also, make sure your derivation script details *why* the sine components cancel out, or you risk dropping a half-mark right at the opening step.

questions.stem_text:
(a) Use Gauss's law to derive an expression for the electric field due to an infinitely long straight thin wire of uniform linear charge density $\\lambda$.

(b) An infinite line charge produces an electric field of magnitude $9 \\times 10^{4} N/C$ at a distance of $2$ cm in air. Calculate the numerical value of its linear charge density $\\lambda$.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Defining a suitable cylindrical Gaussian surface and proving that flux through its flat end caps is zero.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: Imagine a coaxial cylindrical Gaussian surface of length $l$ and radius $r$. Since $\\vec{E}$ is radial, it runs parallel to the flat end caps ($\\theta = 90^{\\circ}$), making the flux through both flat ends zero.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Evaluating the electric flux integral across the curved surface boundary of the cylinder.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: On the curved surface area, $\\vec{E}$ is perpendicular to the boundary skin everywhere, meaning $\\vec{E}$ and $d\\vec{A}$ are parallel ($\\theta = 0^{\\circ}$). Flux is $\\Phi = E \\cdot (2\\pi r l$).

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Expressing the total enclosed charge and setting up the complete Gauss's law equation link.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: The total net charge enclosed within this cylinder is $q = \\lambda l$. Applying Gauss's law: $E \\cdot (2\\pi r l) = \\frac{\\lambda l}{\\epsilon_{0}}$.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Isolating the electric field variable and stating the final algebraic vector expression.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: Canceling length $l$ from both sides yields $E = \\frac{\\lambda}{2\\pi\\epsilon_{0}r}$. Vector form is $\\vec{E} = \\frac{\\lambda}{2\\pi\\epsilon_{0}r}\\hat{n}$, where $\\hat{n}$ is the radial unit vector.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Solving the numerical calculation for the charge density variable with proper S.I. units.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: Rearranging fields gives $\\lambda = 2\\pi\\epsilon_{0}rE = \\frac{rE}{2k}$. Substituting parameters: $\\lambda = \\frac{0.02 \\times 9 \\times 10^{4}}{2 \\times 9 \\times 10^{9}} = 10^{-7}\\text{ C/m} = 0.1 \\mu{\\text{C/m}}$.

questions.topper_execution_logic: When sketching this diagram on your response sheet, never draw the imaginary Gaussian cylinder with solid lines! Solid lines imply a physical boundary rather than an imaginary surface, which triggers a $-0.5$ mark deduction. Always render your Gaussian surface using clear, dashed lines. Also, remember to convert your distance parameter from centimeters to meters ($2$ cm $= 0.02 m$) or your whole numerical step collapses.

questions.stem_text:
(a) State Gauss's law in electrostatics. Using this law, derive an expression for the electric field intensity due to an infinite thin plane sheet of uniform surface charge density $\\sigma$.

(b) Two large, thin metal plates are parallel and close to each other. On their inner faces, the plates have surface charge densities of opposite signs and of magnitude $17.0 \\times 10^{-22} C/m^{2}$. Calculate the net electric field $E$ between these plates.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Stating Gauss's law completely and detailing a correct closed box Gaussian surface setup across the sheet plane.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: Gauss's law states that total electric flux through a closed surface is $1/\\epsilon_{0}$ times the enclosed charge. Choose a rectangular parallelepiped or cylinder of cross-sectional area A cutting normally through the sheet.
algebraic_progression: $\\text{Gaussian Surface chosen: Rectangular parallelepiped of area A}$

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Evaluating the boundary face integrals to show total flux accumulation over opposite planes.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: Electric field lines run parallel to the side faces, contributing zero flux. Only the two parallel end faces ($1$ and $2$) contribute to flux, adding up vectorially to yield a net flux of $2EA$.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Formulating the enclosed surface charge and isolating the electric field equation.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: The total net charge enclosed within the box boundary is $q = \\sigma A$. Equating by Gauss's law gives $2EA = \\frac{\\sigma A}{\\epsilon_{0}} \\implies E = \\frac{\\sigma}{2\\epsilon_{0}}$.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Providing the formalized vector representation and explicitly noting coordinate independence.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: Vector form is $\\vec{E} = \\frac{\\sigma}{2\\epsilon_{0}}\\hat{n}$. This derivation proves an additional physical fact: the field intensity $E$ is entirely independent of distance $x$ from the plane sheet.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Evaluating the parallel-plate field problem to find the total internal field strength.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: Between opposite plates, fields from both sheets point in the same direction and reinforce each other: $E = \\frac{\\sigma}{2\\epsilon_{0}} + \\frac{\\sigma}{2\\epsilon_{0}} = \\frac{\\sigma}{\\epsilon_{0}}$. Substituting values: $E = \\frac{17.0 \\times 10^{-22}}{8.854 \\times 10^{-12}} \\approx 1.92 \\times 10^{-10}\\text{ N/C}$.

questions.topper_execution_logic: Be super careful when working through the parallel plate numerical calculation in part (b). Because the plates carry *opposite* signs, their individual fields reinforce each other in the interior region, adding up to $E = \\frac{\\sigma}{\\epsilon_{0}}$. If you get confused by the opposite signs and subtract them to get zero, you will instantly lose the final mark. Explicitly state that both field vectors run in the same direction between the plates.

questions.stem_text:
(a) Define an electric dipole and its dipole moment vector $\\vec{p}$, explicitly stating its conventional directional orientation in space.

(b) Derive an expression for the electric field intensity vector of an electric dipole at any point on its axial line at a distance $r$ from its center.

(c) Write the simplified vector formula for the field in its far-field limit ($r \\gg a$) and contrast this spatial dependence with that of a single isolated point charge.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Defining the electric dipole and stating the precise formula and direction of its moment vector.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: An electric dipole is a pair of equal and opposite point charges separated by $2a$. Its dipole moment vector is $\\vec{p} = q \\times 2a \\hat{p}$, pointing conventionally from the negative charge to the positive charge.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Setting up the individual field component equations for the positive and negative charges along the axis layout.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: At an axial point P at distance $r$ from the center on the side of $+q: E_{-q} = -\\frac{q}{4\\pi\\epsilon_{0}(r+a)^{2}}\\hat{p}$ and $E_{+q} = \\frac{q}{4\\pi\\epsilon_{0}(r-a)^{2}}\\hat{p}$.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Executing vector superposition and combining the terms over a common algebraic denominator.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: Total field is the vector sum: $\\vec{E} = \\vec{E}_{+q} + \\vec{E}_{-q} = \\frac{q}{4\\pi\\epsilon_{0}} \\left[ \\frac{1}{(r-a)^{2}} - \\frac{1}{(r+a)^{2}} \\right] \\hat{p}$.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Simplifying the combined terms into the standard full algebraic equation for the axial field.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: Simplifying the numerator terms yields the exact expression for the axial field profile: $\\vec{E} = \\frac{1}{4\\pi\\epsilon_{0}} \\frac{4qar}{(r^{2}-a^{2})^{2}} \\hat{p} = \\frac{1}{4\\pi\\epsilon_{0}} \\frac{2pr}{(r^{2}-a^{2})^{2}} \\hat{p}$.

marking_scheme_breakdown.evaluation_trigger: Stating the far-field approximation limit and contrasting the inverse scaling profiles.
marking_scheme_breakdown.minimum_viable_text: For $r \\gg a$, the equation simplifies to $\\vec{E} = \\frac{2\\vec{p}}{4\\pi\\epsilon_{0}r^{3}}$. This shows an inverse-cube distance dependence ($1/r^{3}$), contrasting with a single charge's inverse-square ($1/r^{2}$) dependence.

questions.topper_execution_logic: Make sure you don't mess up your common denominator subtraction algebra in step $3$! Expanding those terms incorrectly will completely derail your derivation and cost you full points. Also, explicitly highlight the contrast in distance scaling—stating clearly that the dipole field drops off much faster as $1/r^{3}$ due to the partial cancellation of opposite charges at large distances will secure your final point.
